behaviors.description: Rows with missing required fields are rejected and appear in the dead-letter queue with the original row number.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is module handoff: CSV parser/row index -> schema validator -> DLQ writer. A unit test of validator logic alone would miss bugs where row numbers are offset or rejected rows are not persisted to DLQ. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require real network/OS/subprocess (no requirement to hit real S3 or a real queue service), so System is not required.
behaviors.test_description: Run the pipeline in-process with a small CSV fixture containing missing required fields. Assert rejected rows are written to the DLQ store and each entry keeps the original CSV row number.

behaviors.description: The transformation correctly converts date strings in multiple formats (ISO 8601, US, European) to a single canonical format.
behaviors.justification: This is algorithm/parsing logic in the transformer itself. No explicit module-boundary failure is described; fakes do not hide the core failure here. Rejected higher level: behavior text is exclusively about conversion correctness, not storage, retries, or infrastructure interactions.
behaviors.test_description: Unit-test the date-normalization function with representative ISO 8601, US, and European inputs (plus edge cases) and assert exact canonical output.

behaviors.description: A CSV file with 500,000 rows completes processing within the configured timeout without running out of memory.
behaviors.justification: Real failure depends on runtime memory behavior and wall-clock timeout under large load, which are OS/process characteristics. In-process mocks can hide allocator, buffering, and scheduling issues. Rejected lower level: Integration with stubs cannot reliably reproduce OOM/timeout behavior.
behaviors.test_description: Execute the real pipeline process against a 500k-row fixture under production-like timeout settings; measure completion time and peak memory, asserting completion before timeout and no OOM/crash.

behaviors.description: When the S3 bucket returns a transient 503 error, the pipeline retries with exponential backoff rather than failing the entire batch.
behaviors.justification: Primary risk is orchestration between pipeline control flow and S3 client error handling/backoff policy. This can be reproduced with an in-process S3 client stub returning 503 then success. Rejected higher level: behavior text does not require validating real network transport quirks, only retry/backoff behavior for a 503 response.
behaviors.test_description: Wire pipeline + retry policy + stub S3 client in-process. Configure stub to return 503 for first N attempts then success; assert retry count, exponential backoff schedule, and eventual batch continuation.

behaviors.description: Rows successfully written to PostgreSQL are not duplicated if the pipeline is restarted mid-batch.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary includes process lifecycle (restart) plus durable DB state across runs. Real restart semantics and commit/idempotency behavior are best exposed with a real process and PostgreSQL instance; pure in-process tests can miss crash/restart edge cases. Rejected higher level: no AI/LLM dependency, so Agentic/Workflow are disqualified by behavior text.
behaviors.test_description: Start pipeline against real PostgreSQL, force interruption mid-batch after some rows commit, restart pipeline, then assert final table has no duplicate logical rows and total row count matches expected once.
